data.title: China to cut key policy relending rates
data.description:
China’s central bank will slash key policy relending rates by a quarter of a percentage point, its deputy governor announced at Thursday’s press conference in Beijing.
The one-year rate of the People’s Bank of China’s structural relending tools will be lowered to 1.25 per cent from the current 1.5 per cent, Zou Lan, the deputy governor said.
More to follow ...

data.title: China’s SpacemiT to launch server-class RISC-V processor after raising US$86 million
data.description:
Chinese semiconductor start-up SpacemiT, fresh off an over US$86 million funding round, is gearing up for mass production of its new RISC-V chips, using an architecture rivalling Intel’s and Arm’s, which is seen as Beijing’s hope for achieving chip self-sufficiency.
The Hangzhou-based company recently completed a series B funding round raising more than 600 million yuan from investors, including fellow Hangzhou firm Huaxia Hengtian, the Beijing Artificial Intelligence Industry Investment Fund and Hong Kong’s Brizan Ventures.
The capital injection will help accelerate research and development of the company’s next-generation RISC-V AI chips and strengthen the firm’s talent pool, as it pins high hopes on the rapid rise of artificial intelligence to drive wider adoption of the open-source architecture, according to SpacemiT.
“We are firmly optimistic about AI and large language models, and that’s why we are driving the intelligent upgrade [with RISC-V-based chips],” Chen Zhijian, founder and CEO of SpacemiT, told the Post last Thursday.
With SpacemiT’s chips designed to support the deployment of large language models, Chen is betting on the rise of Edge AI – devices that can run AI algorithms locally rather than in the cloud – to boost the adoption of RISC-V chips.

K3, the company’s latest RISC-V AI central processing unit, would start mass production this April, after completing tape-out – the final step in semiconductor design before going into production – last year, Chen said.
Chen declined to reveal which wafer foundry partner would produce the company’s chips.
The company’s first server-class processor would also make its debut in the fourth quarter of 2026, when it would be applied on a small scale in several data centres, Chen said.
SpacemiT launched its first octa-core 64-bit RISC-V AI chip K1 in 2024, and has shipped over 150,000 units, with applications in open-source hardware, AI-enabled devices and robotics.
“Edge AI-enabled devices will be the most important pillar [of our company’s revenue],” Chen said, citing smart home devices as one of the key growth areas for its RISC-V chips.
RISC-V refers to the fifth generation of the open-standard reduced instruction set computer architecture. Unlike rivals such as Intel’s x86, a complex instruction set that dominates personal computers, and Arm’s eponymous proprietary RISC-inspired architecture, which dominates the smartphone market, RISC-V is free for anyone to design and customise CPUs.
Beijing is banking on RISC-V as one of the country’s best hopes for achieving chip self-sufficiency amid the tech war with the US, which has denied some Chinese companies access to advanced chips from Nvidia and foundry services from leading wafer fabs such as Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co.
While RISC-V was still in the early stages of adoption, research firm Omdia forecast that shipments of processors based on the architecture would grow 50 per cent each year between 2024 and 2030, reaching 17 billion units in 2030.
SpacemiT is competing with a crop of tech giants and start-ups that are ramping up investments in RISC-V chips, including e-commerce giant Alibaba Group Holding.

In February, Alibaba’s research arm Damo Academy launched the C930 server-grade CPU, the latest addition to its XuanTie RISC-V processor series. Alibaba owns the Post.
Chen said he wanted to create an ecosystem of both chips and its software platform before the market entered the consolidation stage, which he anticipated would take place in four to five years.
The company would work on attracting top developers to use SpacemiT products and provide feedback to the platform, Chen said. “Building the ecosystem will be one of SpacemiT’s most important tasks going forward,” he said.

data.title: The founders of Hong Kong’s Nectere Watches on their first collection and lessons learned
data.description:
When independent brands Anoma and Berneron ushered in a wave of distinctly asymmetrical case designs in 2024, there was one more watch brand that was meant to be in their ranks. Initially conceived in 2023, Nectere’s first collection – Order From Chaos – was launched following an April 2025 Kickstarter campaign that to date has totalled over HK$690,000 (US$88,700), against a goal of HK$52,000 (US$6,680), with the first orders fulfilled in September 2025.
Co-founders James Wong and Tony Yip are childhood friends, having been at junior school together. “We always had a common interest,” Wong says as we sit down to speak with him about the brand. “We used to collect trainers, then cameras, watches and even furniture.” Their common thread is a love of design.
According to Nectere’s materials, the Order From Chaos specifically takes cues from visual representations of chaos theory, a branch of mathematics that explores number systems whose patterns and outcomes can be radically affected by the slightest shifts in inputs. However, Wong shares that the inspiration goes even further back.

“We were having dinner together,” Wong recalls. “At first we were thinking of launching our own app, doing something more digital. But I noticed an oyster shell, and suddenly wanted to start a design based on how random [the lines on an] oyster shell are. That’s how we came to the chaos theory, because we can’t call the watch ‘Oyster’ since in the watch industry, an Oyster is a diver’s watch.”
Wong and Yip’s contrasting design tastes came together into something like harmony – or order, if you will. The Order From Chaos collection consequently comes in three dial colours – salmon, green and silver – housed in an asymmetrical lugless case with a stepped bezel.
“Tony is more of a trends guy,” Wong explains. “He’s very good at identifying what’s going to be a big trend in the market. He can identify potential designs. I’m more old school. Because I like older brands, I care less about trends, so the difference between us is a good combination.” It was Wong’s idea to do a salmon dial due to its roots in vintage watch designs, whereas it was Yip’s idea to do a green dial.

Executing their design came with challenges. Nectere engaged a third designer to develop the watch, which required multiple rounds of 3D printing to get right, according to Wong. The suppliers found it challenging to cut sapphire crystals too, but back then Chinese factories were willing to try new things at lower quantities given the downturn at the time. This happily coincided with Chinese factories showing improving aptitude for watchmaking. There were also fewer language barriers.
The Order From Chaos is powered by the Seagull ST17 hand-wound movement from the Sea-Gull factory in Tianjin, whose first movements were developed after being tasked by the Chinese government with acquiring the then-luxury Venus 175 Swiss chronograph movement for use in military-issue watches in 1955. Today, the factory supplies movements for microbrands like Studio Underd0g, which used one of its calibres inside the colourful Av0cado and Guacam0le models, among its popular visuals-focused watches.
“People who are not in the industry think Chinese watchmaking is cheap and not very promising,” Wong explains. “In the market, you see brands like Atelier Wen – a good example of how Chinese watchmaking can reach an international audience. As long as there are microbrands trying to get into the industry using Chinese factories, these factories will improve their production skills.”

Despite the promising initial signs, Nectere’s production and launch fell prey to wider market forces almost until the end. “It was funny because we first launched on Kickstarter on April 18 [2025],” Wong recalls. “It was the first week of [President Donald Trump’s] trade war. We were frustrated. US tariffs went up so a lot of people backing the project there refunded because they didn’t want to pay so much.” At the time, Wong even considered moving production to Taiwan to incur lower tariffs, but then Trump’s pause on increases until August 2025 helped Nectere keep production in China for this run.
The watches were priced at US$575 for Kickstarter backers and around US$725 for pre-orders. As of October 2025, Nectere notified its followers on Instagram that the watches would incur import tariffs of around US$150 when shipping from Hong Kong, and collaborated with a freight forwarder to reduce the cost to just US$30 for a US buyer.

Nectere is facing these challenges at a time when Hong Kong is seeing its own smaller independent watch brands emerge globally. Metrical Watches and Earthen Co. are just two local brands to have also launched a watch this year, specialising in a one-hand configuration and affordable ceramic construction, respectively. Then Behrens from Shenzhen was named as a semi-finalist for the 2025-26 Louis Vuitton Watch Prize for Independent Creatives in July.
Even so, Wong cautions aspiring watch brand founders. “Think differently, do new designs or pick a new niche in which to do a watch,” he advises. “You also have to look out for the cost of marketing nowadays. Ad spending, all the PR, digital marketing – these are very expensive. Make sure you have calculated it all.”
Wong prefers an organic approach where possible: “We work with communities. For example, in Thailand, I met up with a few people from a watch club … and then we go round, get a beer or something.”

“I think we have a wide range of customers,” Wong says. “We have diehard collectors that really love our design, and we also have customers who haven’t bought a watch online [before]. The design is fun. And to me, this is definitely not your only watch. If you buy a Nectere, you’re probably going to already have at least three to five watches in your wardrobe.”
It’s the veteran collector’s appreciation for Nectere’s design that’s driven the brand’s success. The Order From Chaos salmon dial, or Dune, was submitted to Le Grand Prix d’Horlogerie de Genève (GPHG) by anonymous members of the GPHG Academy, though it didn’t make the list of 2025 finalists. Around that time, Wong and Yip went to Toronto as Timepiece World Journal had selected the Dune as a finalist in its Timepiece World Awards, though it ultimately did not make the winners list.
“It’s hard to get an award,” Wong says. “But I think being a new brand nominated by collectors or the jury from a board – that’s already a win for us. Even if we can’t get the award at the end, it’s already a huge milestone. It’s also a very good chance for us to meet collectors overseas. I think for us, we’ve already won.”

data.title: Nexperia and Chinese owner fight for control of chipmaker in Dutch court
data.description:
The battle for control of Dutch chipmaker Nexperia entered a new phase on Wednesday, as lawyers representing the firm and its Chinese parent Wingtech Technology traded blows during a seven-hour court hearing in the Netherlands.
The Enterprise Chamber – a special Dutch court handling corporate disputes – scheduled the hearing to determine whether it should order a formal investigation into alleged mismanagement at Nexperia or overturn its earlier ruling, with a verdict set to be issued by February 11.
The court played a key role in suspending Wingtech’s control of Nexperia in October, when it issued a provisional ruling removing former CEO Zhang Xuezheng and transferring the voting rights on shares indirectly owned by Wingtech to an independent administrator.
That decision – which came soon after the Dutch government’s seizure of Nexperia – sparked a months-long tussle over the company’s fate that raised tensions with Beijing and threatened the supply chains of European carmakers.
During the hearing, the two sides sparred over the cause of the crisis, whether ex-CEO Zhang planned to erode Nexperia’s operations in Europe, and to what extent the supply chain disruptions that followed his removal should be seen as retaliation.
Lawyers representing Zhang and Wingtech argued the crisis stemmed from a rushed and unjustified intervention by the Dutch government and the Enterprise Chamber, while Nexperia’s legal team maintained the blame lay with Zhang’s mismanagement.
The Dutch side’s intervention was carried out based on “one-sided”, “misleading” and “false” information provided by Nexperia and had led to “disastrous” consequences for everyone involved, according to Wingtech’s legal team.
Zhang’s lawyer stressed the way the intervention was carried out was “incomprehensible” to Zhang, as well as to many people within the company.
To illustrate his point, the lawyer cited an email sent by Jean-Pierre Kempeneers – Nexperia’s chief corporate affairs at the time and a veteran diplomat – to other company executives shortly after the Dutch interventions.
“What the f … is happening? Why? Why this way? Why this timing, coinciding with the BIS announcement?” he wrote, referring to the US Bureau of Industry and Security’s decision to expand its trade blacklists in late September.


The BIS move could have led to Nexperia being subjected to US sanctions, as the new rules stated that any company at least 50 per cent owned by entities on its trade blacklists would face restrictions. Wingtech was blacklisted in late 2024.
Wingtech’s legal team also questioned Nexperia’s claim that an intervention was urgently needed because the company was being emptied out, noting that the company had US$396 million in its bank account at the time.
Zhang, who has largely disappeared from public view since the saga erupted, was absent from the hearing. The events of the past few months have affected him “deeply”, according to his lawyer.
On the other side, Nexperia’s lawyers reiterated the company’s accusation that Zhang had been moving Nexperia’s operations from Europe to China under the pretext of a plan known as Project Rainbow.
Zhang had explored the possibility of selling Nexperia’s European factories by the end of 2025 and eventually firing 45 per cent of Nexperia’s staff in Europe as part of Project Rainbow, lawyers representing the Dutch company said.
Wingtech and Zhang’s team argued that Project Rainbow was a legitimate contingency plan created as the company faced the risk of being added to a US blacklist, which had been supported by the entire executive team.
Many of the measures associated with Project Rainbow – such as a proposal to sell Nexperia’s factories in Hamburg and Manchester – were simply options that had been assessed for feasibility and eventually dropped, the legal team said.
Nexperia also accused Zhang of using its resources to support WingSkySemi (WSS), a Shanghai-based wafer maker founded and owned by Zhang. The former CEO had asked Nexperia to increase its orders from WSS even when it went against the company’s interests, according to the firm’s legal team.
Meanwhile, spontaneous debates erupted between the legal teams throughout the hearing on issues such as whose client should take the initiative to resolve the deadlock over the company’s fate.
Both sides expressed a desire to resolve the issue as quickly as possible. However, as the exchanges so far have shown, the path forward remains challenging.

data.title: China make history by advancing to quarter-finals of AFC U-23 Asian Cup for first time
data.description:
China’s under-23 football team made history by advancing to the quarter-finals of the AFC U-23 Asian Cup for the first time after drawing 0-0 with Thailand in their final Group D match on Wednesday.
The result left China second in the group with one win and two draws, behind Australia, who topped the standings on goal difference after beating Iraq 2-1. The group games were played in Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
China will face Uzbekistan in the quarter-finals in Jeddah on Saturday.
China’s campaign started with a 0-0 draw against Iraq, followed by a 1-0 win over Australia.
The Chinese football team have participated in all seven editions of the tournament since its inception in 2013, but had never made it out of the group stage.

Under-23 head coach Antonio Puche Vicente praised his players’ belief after the match.
“I am very happy and so proud of my players. They were fantastic and believed we could qualify – that belief is so important in football. Believe, believe, believe,” the Spaniard said.
“The most important thing is we have advanced to the next stage and we are going to Jeddah. It’s a great new beginning for us. They deserve to celebrate this moment today – you have to enjoy these moments on the pitch.
“Tomorrow we head to Jeddah to prepare for Uzbekistan. It will be tough, but as long as we’re in this competition, we have a chance.”
Captain Xu Bin was relishing the challenge of facing Uzbekistan.
“We have faced this Uzbekistan team twice before and they are very strong at the Asian level, but we have confidence in ourselves. We just need to do our best,” he said.
Vicente added: “This is a great challenge for us because we’ve never reached this stage in our history. Now we are in the last eight and we need to keep working hard and give our maximum effort.
“For me, the most significant thing is that China needs to cherish this moment. Tomorrow we’ll continue preparing, but believe me – enjoy this moment.”
Uzbekistan’s under-23 side have a strong record in the competition, winning one title, finishing second twice and securing a fourth-place finish in the past four editions.

data.title: China’s Ningbo cuisine; Hong Kong goes Back to the Past: 7 Lifestyle highlights
data.description:
We have selected seven Lifestyle stories from the past seven days that resonated with our readers. If you would like to see more of our reporting, please consider subscribing.
1. ‘Umami, salty, stinky’: how 3 flavours define China’s Ningbo cuisine
Located directly south of Shanghai across Hangzhou Bay, Ningbo is just a two-hour ride away from China’s biggest city on the high-speed rail. For many, the first thing that may come to mind is the city’s famous tangyuan (glutinous rice dumplings). But while pantries in the two cities may share a few key ingredients, Ningbo’s cuisine – blessed with a natural bounty from mountain, river and sea – reflects different flavours compared with its more famous neighbour.
2. Singaporean dementia study links Alzheimer’s to clogged ‘brain drains’
You might have heard of “brain drain” in the context of talented and well-educated people leaving their own country to seek advancement in another. You may not have heard about the brain drains in your head.
3. Year of the Horse 2026 predictions for all 12 zodiac signs. Is your luck in?

Renowned fortune-teller Tong Pik-ha offers her comprehensive predictions for the year, delving into the celestial influences that will shape the destinies of those born under each Chinese zodiac sign.
4. What the Chinese surname Ma, meaning ‘horse’, symbolises and how it spread

There have been many influential figures with the surname Ma throughout history, from the Han dynasty army general Ma Yuan and American-born cellist prodigy Yo-Yo Ma to Alibaba founder Jack Ma Yun and Tencent chief executive Pony Ma.
5. Why are Korean-style cafes proving so popular in Hong Kong?
Hong Kong’s culinary scene is a relentless parade of the new, a dizzying cycle of pop-ups and passing fads. Yet few trends have been as noticeable as the rise of the Korean-style cafe.
6. How Back to the Past is fuelling nostalgia for 2000s Hong Kong

The 2000s are experiencing a nostalgia-driven renaissance in Hong Kong, spearheaded by the record-breaking time-travel action fantasy, Back to the Past. A big-screen sequel to the popular 2001 TVB drama A Step into the Past, the film has become a box office sensation since its release on December 31, 2025.
7. This Chinese-Vietnamese plant-based influencer makes even haters love tofu
January used to be about detoxes and gym sign-ups. Now, for millions, it is about giving up meat, eggs and dairy – at least for a while, as more people taste-test the vegan lifestyle.

data.title: Malaysia’s super-rich get cold feet as Langkawi luxury car tax perks vanish
data.description:
For the very rich, a dream car like a Ferrari, Lamborghini or Porsche used to cost 2.5 million ringgit (US$617,240) in Langkawi, a duty-free island in Malaysia’s Kedah state. They will now cost about 5 million ringgit.
Industry players say the federal government’s move to abolish tax exemptions for luxury vehicles priced above 300,000 ringgit in Langkawi is expected to hurt the automotive sector and reduce long-term government revenue.
Industry veteran Mohamad Azhar Mohd Jamil said the decision would affect dealers and weaken tax collection over time.
“About 90 per cent of Ferrari cars and several other luxury brands in Malaysia were registered in Langkawi because of its tax-free status,” he said.
If luxury vehicles could no longer be sold, he said, the government would lose corporate tax and customs-related revenue.
“Even after three or four years, vehicle owners are still required to pay customs-related charges and corporate tax every year. With this move, the government would suffer losses from the start,” he said.
Mohamad Azhar also said there are still no clear guidelines on how the policy would be implemented.
He said the uncertainty was already being felt, with dealers reporting cancellations.
“I have many friends who are dealers and they were complaining that a large number of vehicle bookings had been cancelled this month because of the decision,” he added.

Another industry veteran, Ong Seng Aun, urged the government to review the move, warning it could have wider economic consequences.
He said the tax-free incentive had made Langkawi an attractive venue for luxury car brands to hold global product launches, drawing customers and enthusiasts from around the world.
“These events stimulated tourism and benefited hotels and local businesses. The spillover effect created multiple economic benefits for Langkawi,” he said.
Ong said the presence of luxury vehicles on the island generated steady spillover revenue as owners required the cars to be properly maintained, securely stored when they were away and customised locally, creating ongoing business for specialised workshops, logistics operators and service providers.
“High-net-worth individuals often bought luxury vehicles in Langkawi for leisure use on the island,” he said.
“Those intending to bring vehicles out of the island were required to provide a bank guarantee to customs, which safeguarded government interests while allowing flexibility for owners.”
Ong also said the existing mechanism had been effective in regulating the movement of tax-free vehicles.
“Removing the incentive could weaken Langkawi’s appeal as a luxury automotive hub,” he said, urging the authorities to engage industry players before finalising the policy.
A Ferrari previously cost between 800,000 ringgit and 1.5 million ringgit, depending on the model and specifications, while brands such as Lamborghini and Porsche could reach 2.5 million ringgit.
Without tax exemptions, buyers are now expected to pay about 100 per cent more.
Kedah Chamber of Commerce and Industry committee member Edison Ooi said the move could have wide-ranging effects on Langkawi’s economy, affecting logistics, hotels and the broader tourism ecosystem.
“Many people could lose their jobs as the industry generated significant employment opportunities across the island,” he said.
Ooi said residents were not opposed to change but any new measures must bring clear benefits to the community.
“We are ready to support and collaborate with the government. What we need is a well-thought-out plan that supports the island’s economy,” he said.
He suggested that maintaining tax exemptions for supercars, which continued to attract high-value tourists and investors, would help sustain jobs and encourage investment in luxury services and hospitality.
During the tabling of budget 2026, Prime Minister Anwar Ibrahim said luxury vehicles valued above 300,000 ringgit in Langkawi and Labuan would no longer enjoy vehicle tax exemptions from this year.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Mapping issues in 7 of 8 power bank rental platforms tested by Hong Kong watchdog
data.description:
Seven out of eight power bank rental platforms tested by Hong Kong’s consumer watchdog displayed inaccurate navigation or positioning data on map applications, a problem that may waste users’ time and incur extra charges due to delays.
The Consumer Council on Thursday also found a significant variance in rental fees across different stations within the same platforms, with charges for a 30-minute rental differing by nearly 1.3 times and the daily cap on charges differing by more than 2.3 times.
“When in urgent need of a power bank, consumers’ phone battery is often critically low. Inaccurate navigation not only wastes time, but may also deplete the phone’s battery, preventing the user from logging in to use the service,” said the watchdog’s chief executive Alaina Shum Jiu-fai.
“Incorrect station data during return could lead to fruitless trips and extra charges due to delays.”
Shum said that as smartphones were an important part of daily life, running out of battery while out and about had become a common problem for city residents, with shared power bank rental platforms offering a “flexible solution” to the issue.
Staff from the Consumer Council conducted 48 rent and return trials across the eight platforms in Hong Kong between November and December in 2025, with the tests covering different days, time slots and districts.
“Although all eight platforms had positioning and third-party map navigation functions, seven of them experienced issues such as reversed navigation routes, target stations deviating from actual locations, or inaccurate starting points,” Shum said.
“Four platforms operating via mini-programmes had more stable performance when using certain map systems, but were less accurate with two other third-party systems.”
For four other platforms which operated mainly on their own apps, Shum said they worked better on third-party map systems.
Among the tests, one of the worst-performing platforms was HiCharge, which successfully guided the tester to their destination four out of six times when using the Amap application, but failed all six navigation tests using Google Maps and Apple Maps. The platform operated through a mini-programme on WeChat.
Amap is operated by Alibaba Group Holding, which also owns the South China Morning Post.
The council also found inaccuracies in power bank station information in three of their tests on HiCharge, including incorrect coordinates and incomplete addresses.
One of the best-performing platforms was CHARGESPOT, which mainly operates within its own mobile application. It successfully guided testers to their power bank stations all six times using Google Maps and Apple Maps, with accurate location information.
Responding to the council, HiCharge said it would strengthen its coordination and frequency of updates. It said inaccuracies in information were partly due to difficulties in controlling the operating hours of its partner stores.
The platform explained that the deviation in their navigation was due to technical limitations, as well as differences caused by the conversion of map latitude and longitude. It said that it had provided addresses to help users find the location of their stations.
Shum noted that operators could improve in three areas: strengthening their coordination with third-party maps, providing clearer supplementary guidance information in complex environments, and regularly verifying and updating their station information.

data.title: Japan woman mocked for ‘ugly’ looks, resists cosmetic surgery, transforms with make-up
data.description:
A 50-year-old Japanese woman, who was mocked for being “ugly” as a child, has shunned cosmetic surgery and spent 13 years taking more than 30,000 selfies to study make-up, skincare and fashion.
The ridiculing left Kozue Sakurada with deep emotional scars, but her persistence has inspired countless people online.
A male classmate once stared at her and repeated the word “ugly” to her face 10 times.

Other boys even made up a parody of a children’s song to mock her large nose.
By her early twenties, the trauma was so intense that if she saw someone covering their mouth to laugh on a train, she assumed they were laughing at her and would get off at the next stop in embarrassment.
The turning point came at the age of 37, after a heartbreaking break-up. Her then-boyfriend once looked at her and said: “If only you could be a bit prettier.”
Although she began trying to change her appearance to suit his preferences, about six months later, he still broke up with her.

It was then that Sakurada realised she wanted to become beautiful for herself.
Instead of turning to cosmetic surgery, she studied fashion trends through magazines and social media, and began taking daily full-body selfies in the mirror to analyse her appearance and seek improvement.
She experimented with different styles, adjusting skirt lengths by a centimetre at a time and trying various camisole necklines to see what best suited her body.
“It felt like conducting a scientific study,” she said.

To better understand what worked for her, she even underwent a “skeletal analysis” and concluded that her frame and facial features did not suit cute, girlie dresses.
Instead, casual styles like shirts and trousers suited her more.
However, at age 40, Sakurada suffered a breakdown during a psychology lecture.
Then, by revisiting her childhood hobby of sewing clothes, she finally began to heal.

“Even though I was changing my outfits, I still felt ugly deep inside,” she said.
“During a psychology workshop, I tried on a skirt I had made myself and felt it looked better than expected. I was genuinely happy. That moment made me realise that while other people’s opinions matter, what is most important is how I feel. I should wear what I want.”
Over 13 years, Sakurada took more than 30,000 selfies to study how to look more beautiful.
Eventually, she came to understand: “The key is not to lose yourself in the pursuit of other people’s approval.”
“I believe the true secret to beauty lies in the heart. The more we accept ourselves, the more beautiful we become. I still have room to grow on this journey of self-improvement, and I want to cherish that feeling and enjoy the process.”

Her story has inspired many people online.
One Japanese netizen wrote: “Rather than her physical features changing, I believe it is the result of her getting to know herself better. She changed in a beautiful way. Her beauty is the product of her own hard work: natural, not manufactured.”
A Chinese viewer said: “She is so beautiful now, it is like she glows. What is amazing is that she never took the short cut of plastic surgery. Truly admirable.”
A third person added: “This is incredible, not just a transformation in appearance, but a redemption of the soul. She pulled herself out of the swamp of self-denial.”

data.title: How the Osaka and Kyoto Ritz-Carltons blend classic luxury with Japanese tradition
data.description:
Whenever I visit a new destination, I am always in two minds when it comes to booking a hotel.
On the one hand, I want the familiar – the unapologetically indulgent trappings of luxury travel. In my experience, that means a classically Western style. Give me the marble and brass, mahogany and velvet. After a long day spent exploring new lands, I yearn to return to the comfort of a timeless, brand-scented lobby.
On the other hand, I want the property to belong to its address. I want the storytelling, the elevation of local artisans. I want – to use that deplorable travel cliché – an “authentic” experience. On a recent trip to vibrant Osaka and its quieter, historic neighbour Kyoto, I was able to enjoy both experiences over a long weekend courtesy of the Ritz-Carlton.

The Osaka property is located in the city’s Umeda district, a bustling business and shopping hub. Taking its cue from César Ritz’s late 19th‑ and early 20th‑century grand hotels in London and Paris, the hotel is opulent indeed. It opened in May 1997 as the first Ritz-Carlton hotel in Japan, and a 2024 renovation has modernised the amenities in the almost 300 rooms while maintaining the classic European style that is particularly popular with domestic travellers.
Contributing to the sense of timeless grandeur is around 450 works of art, most of which are oil paintings, and interiors in an 18th-century English style. The hotel’s restaurants are also true to the theme, with Michelin-starred French restaurant La Baie and Italian restaurant Splendido both leaning into classics and culinary tradition.

The hotel’s Japanese restaurant Hanagatami is organised into five areas, each with its own menu dedicated to a different facet of Japanese culinary culture: sushi, charcoal grill, kaiseki, teppanyaki and tempura. During my trip, the now-ended Flavors in Transit menu brought chefs from all five kitchens together to present a unique tasting menu inspired by railway station bento boxes. In the city known as the “kitchen of Japan”, Hanagatami offers guests the opportunity to tour the pantry without ever leaving the building.
The hotel’s Club Lounge also offers guests opportunities to explore Japanese cultural traditions, with daily Japanese tea ceremonies led by expert tea masters and interactive workshops teaching the Japanese art of furoshiki, wrapping gifts in cloth.

Neighbouring Kyoto is just 15 minutes away from Osaka by bullet train, but the smaller city couldn’t feel more different from the commercial energy of Osaka. Kyoto’s Ritz-Carlton property offers a similar departure in style and tone.
Opened in 2014, the hotel is situated on the bank of the serene Kamo River, overlooking the Higashiyama mountains. Its environs are reflected in the stunning entrance – an enveloping water feature that gently cascades down dozens of stone steps, culminating in a three-storey waterfall that ends at the hotel’s indoor pool and spa. The entrance sets the tone for everything within: a property inspired by its tranquil waterfront setting in the heart of Japan’s cultural capital.

Taking cues from a traditional ryokan, elements of Japanese design are abundantly evident in the hotel. Ribbed wood and grid patterns evoke traditional machiya town houses, while lacquer and rich embroidered silks are used throughout the property too.
The hotel’s collection of over 400 traditional and contemporary artworks – from delicate ceramics to dramatic textiles – features mostly Kyoto-based artists. Many works echo scenes, characters and garden settings from 11th-century novel The Tale of Genji, tying the interiors to Kyoto’s thousand-year literary heritage.

With light wood accents, muted textiles and floor-to-ceiling windows overlooking mountains, the guest rooms give an initial alpine chalet vibe. A closer look at every meticulously crafted detail, however, centres you firmly in the centuries-old city, inviting you to wrap yourself in one of the room’s traditional yukata robes and dive in. Take a soak in the deep tub, making use of the handmade soap from Japanese artisanal soapmaker Shabonya; brew a hot cup of hojicha, sourced from a Kyoto teahouse; settle into the cosy reading nook near the window – modelled after an engawa wooden veranda – and take in the view.
Dining options include Mizuki, which offers kaiseki, sushi, tempura and teppanyaki. Its lunchtime bento box of Kyoto-inspired obanzai small dishes presents an accessible, enjoyable way to sample the local flavours. In addition to the Lobby Lounge and Italian restaurant La Locanda, Chef’s Table by Katsuhito Inoue presents a kaiseki experience – a multi-course journey emphasising seasonal ingredients and artful presentation – inspired by Kyoto’s 72 micro-seasons.

Kyoto’s famous Gion geisha district is a 15-minute riverside walk away. The hotel also offers guided tours of some of Kyoto’s iconic spots. I spent my final morning on a dawn cycling tour to the Fushimi Inari-taisha shrine. Watching the sun rise over the Shinto shrine’s vermilion torii gates was an unforgettable end to a trip that offered both the comfort of polish and the thrill of place – a reminder that the best luxury doesn’t insulate you from a destination, it brings you closer to it.
The author stayed as a guest of The Ritz-Carlton Osaka and The Ritz-Carlton Kyoto.
